text: Hi, I'm Vahad McCauley. I'm from Portland, Oregon. And today I'll be sharing the video for the robotic portions of a hybrid unilateral transversus abdominis release ventral hernia repair.

This case was performed on a forty three year old man who had multi recurrent hernias. He had previously had primary repairs as well as mesh based repairs. He presented initially with just an M three defect, six by five centimeters.

Um, but unfortunately in the time between our consultation and the eventual operation, his prior epigastric hernia had recurred. So the operation actually looked more like an M two M three defect with the, with being as wide as eight, he had a bunch of scars. He wanted excise. So you really wanted a hybrid approach.
So that's a CT scan that I had during the clinic consultation. You can see in the epigastrium that mesh, but at the time there wasn't really a hernia.

Hybrid retromuscular is an option I use for select patients who I feel can derive some benefits from a minimally invasive approach while also gaining some advantages for open techniques such as skin and soft tissue reconstruction.

So let's roll the video. Let's start with the crossover. Throughout this video, I'll just sort of highlight the orientation of our camera to the abdominal wall to help hopefully orient you to where we are and what we're doing.

The surgeon begins the crossover procedure by selecting the falciform fat pad as a safe starting point, where there is a healthy amount of preperitoneal fat. A generous opening is made in the posterior rectus sheath, following a dissection in the left retro-rectus space.

The surgeon identifies the delineation between the patient's linea alba and the contralateral posterior sheath, noting the ease of crossover due to the small diastasis in the superior aspect of the abdominal wall.
The surgeon meticulously removes tissue from the fascia before making a precise incision and extending it further.

The surgeon extends the incision and focuses on developing a broad retromuscular dissection on the contralateral side, anticipating the early encounter with the hernia sac due to the patient's M2 hernia.
to reduce the potential of losing visualization if we were to enter the hernia sac or go intraperitoneal.
The surgeon encounters the hernia sac, which appears visually different from the preoperative CT scan, aligning more closely with the findings from the bedside examination on the day of surgery.
The surgeon bluntly dissects a thin hernia sac, inadvertently creating a hole. Despite this error, the previously developed space maintains adequate visualization, allowing the procedure to progress.

The surgical team reduces the hernia sac and identifies a previously placed mesh, which is visible and poorly integrated with the surrounding tissue. The decision is made to explant the mesh due to its poor integration.
The surgeon identifies a bridge of intact fascia, preparing to approach the M-III hernia as previously observed on CT scans.

The surgeon removes a poorly integrated mesh from the retromuscular space with ease, revealing significant scar tissue from a previous procedure. The dissection in the contralateral retro-rectal space is visibly challenging due to the presence of scar tissue.
The surgeon identifies a band of tissue likely related to a prior trans-fascial stitch and works to locate an avascular plane for dissection.

The surgeon navigates through the surgical field, distinguishing between scar tissue and neurovascular bundles. The wide rectus width of approximately twelve centimeters on the right side allows for lateral dissection, revealing predominantly scar tissue.
The surgeon enters an untouched plane, making progress by removing tissue and pushing away fat while preserving the neurovascular bundles.
Right here, there was some confusion, at least for me, intraoperatively about how lateral I could go. So I said, I'll leave it alone until later.

The surgeon unifies the inferior dissection, extending it approximately four to five centimeters initially, and then further extending it to the pubis level during the open procedure for mesh explant and scar excision.

The surgeon presents the original EHSM-III hernia, measuring approximately five by eight centimeters, alongside the larger recurrent M-II hernia, measuring eight by eight centimeters. The surgical field appears tight, prompting the decision to perform a unilateral transversus abdominis release (TAR).
The surgeon identifies the transversus abdominis muscle, noting the presence of medial perforators and staying medial to them during the dissection.

The surgeon transects the posterior lamella of the internal abdominal oblique and systematically cuts through the transversus abdominis muscle, exposing the transversalis fascia and posterior sheath. The semilunar line is clearly identified, ensuring the dissection remains lateral for easier release while staying medial to the neurovascular bundles.

The surgeon dissects the muscle from the transversalis fascia, creating a retromuscular pocket. Scar tissue from previous operations is visible, and some dissection transitions into a pre-peritoneal space.
The surgeon highlights the aponeurotic components of the inferior transversus abdominis while performing cave techniques, carefully staying medial to the visible muscle bundles.

The surgeon progresses with the dissection, revealing the rectus muscle and fat overlying the neurovascular bundles. The transected transversus abdominis muscle is visible, and the lateral dissection is developed, showing both retromuscular and preperitoneal spaces. The healthy space created provides a view of the initiation of a robotic transverse abdominis release.

The surgeon excises the scar tissue and places a 30x30 cm macroporous polypropylene mesh over the surgical site. The anterior layer is closed using interrupted figure-of-eight sutures, and several drains are left in place. The patient is prepared for discharge.

I think hybrid approaches are a great approach for abdominal wall reconstruction. A lot of the benefits of MIS and OPEN combined in one operation. can reduce length of stay, hospitalization costs, and reduce wound complications. And I think it can be a nice bridge as you start applying more complex abdominal wall to your practice.
